name: Valencian Community
history: The Valencian Community has a rich history shaped by its location on Spain's eastern Mediterranean coast. The region was founded as a kingdom in the 13th century by James I of Aragon after the Reconquista, and it became a major center of trade and commerce during the medieval period. Valencia's golden age came during the 15th and 16th centuries when it rivaled Barcelona as a Mediterranean power, though it later declined under Spanish Habsburg rule. The region experienced significant development in the 18th and 19th centuries and today remains one of Spain's most economically dynamic areas.
culturalNotes: The Valencian Community is famous for its vibrant festivals, most notably Las Fallas in March, where elaborate satirical floats are paraded and burned in celebration of spring. The region is deeply rooted in agricultural traditions, particularly rice cultivation in the Turia riverbed, which inspired the world-renowned paella dish. Valencian culture blends Mediterranean influences with a strong sense of regional identity, evident in its distinctive architecture, local cuisine, and the widespread use of the Valencian language in daily life.
bestTimeToVisit: Spring for Las Fallas festival or fall for beach weather without summer crowds.
languageNotes: The official language is Spanish, but Valencian is widely spoken and co-official in the region, representing a unique Romance language closely related to Catalan. Common Valencian phrases include "Bon dia" for good morning and "Adéu" for goodbye, and learning a few local words is appreciated by residents.

places.name: Valencia
places.description: The vibrant capital of the region, blending a historic old town with the futuristic City of Arts and Sciences. Its mix of Gothic architecture, world-class paella, and lively markets make it an essential stop.
imageQueries: Valencia City of Arts and Sciences

activities.detail: Explore Santiago Calatrava's futuristic complex of museums, an IMAX cinema, an oceanarium, and a science museum set along the old riverbed.
Climb the Gothic-Baroque cathedral's bell tower and see the chalice claimed by some to be the Holy Grail.
Wander this UNESCO-listed 15th-century silk exchange, a masterpiece of civil Gothic architecture with twisting columns.
Browse one of Europe's largest fresh food markets, housed in a stunning Art Nouveau iron-and-glass building.
Walk or cycle through the nine-kilometer park created from the diverted Turia riverbed, connecting much of the city.
Get lost in the narrow medieval streets of the old town, filled with street art, tapas bars, and centuries-old palaces.
Spend an afternoon on this wide sandy city beach lined with seafood restaurants and a lively promenade.
Take a boat ride on this freshwater lagoon south of the city, the birthplace of paella and a haven for birdlife.

food.detail: Try the original version of Spain's famous rice dish, made with rabbit, chicken, and flat green beans rather than seafood.
Sip the sweet, milky tiger-nut drink alongside soft, sugar-glazed pastries at a traditional horchatería.
Sample the local brunch cocktail mixing cava, orange juice, vodka, and gin, often served in a shared pitcher.
Taste this rustic eel stew from the Albufera region, cooked with potatoes, garlic, and paprika.
Order this simple tapa of shredded salt cod and roasted red peppers dressed in olive oil.
Try this noodle-based cousin of paella, typically made with seafood and a rich sofrito base.

culture.detail: Most restaurants don't open for lunch until 2pm and dinner rarely starts before 9pm, so plan meal times accordingly.
If visiting in mid-March, expect fireworks, street parties, and giant satirical effigies burned during this iconic UNESCO-listed festival.
Small shops often close for a few hours in the early afternoon, especially outside the main tourist areas.
Locals traditionally eat paella at midday, often on Sundays, rather than for dinner.

places.name: Alicante
places.description: A sunny coastal city crowned by the imposing Santa Bárbara Castle overlooking the Mediterranean. Its palm-lined Explanada promenade and lively beaches make it a favorite gateway to the Costa Blanca.

activities.detail: Climb or take the free elevator from Postiguet beach up to this hilltop fortress for sweeping views over the city and the Mediterranean.
Walk the famous wave-patterned marble promenade lined with palm trees, street performers, and seaside cafes.
This central sandy beach sits right beneath the castle and is perfect for a quick swim or sunbathing session.
Get lost in the old quarter's narrow, colorful, flower-pot-lined streets climbing up toward the castle.
Explore award-winning exhibits on Iberian, Roman, and medieval history of the region.
Take a short ferry from the harbor to Spain's smallest inhabited island, known for clear waters and a marine reserve.
Sample fresh seafood, local produce, and jamón at this bustling early 20th-century market hall.

activities.text: See the Basilica of Santa María
activities.detail: Step into Alicante's oldest church, built in Gothic style atop a former mosque.

food.detail: A local rice dish cooked in fish and seafood stock, typically served with allioli on the side.
A hard almond-and-honey nougat that originates from the wider Alicante province, especially Xixona.
A hearty rice dish with rabbit and snails, a staple of inland Alicante cuisine.
Try cured, salted fish like mojama or dried tuna roe, a Mediterranean tradition sold in local markets.
A flatbread topped with tuna, tomato, and peppers, popular as a light snack or tapa.
Pair the cold tiger-nut drink with sweet glazed pastries at a local horchatería.

culture.detail: Lunch is typically eaten around 2-3pm and dinner rarely starts before 9pm, so plan accordingly.
In late June the city holds massive bonfire and fireworks celebrations similar to Valencia's Las Fallas, so expect crowds and noise.

culture.text: Both Spanish and Valencian are spoken
culture.detail: Street signs and announcements often appear in both languages, reflecting the region's bilingual identity.
It's considered impolite to walk around town in swimsuits away from the immediate beachfront area.

places.name: Peñíscola
places.description: A picturesque walled town on a rocky peninsula, topped by the dramatic Papa Luna Castle. Its narrow whitewashed streets and golden beaches have made it a beloved filming location and seaside escape.

activities.detail: Climb the 13th-century castle built by the Knights Templar and later home to Pope Benedict XIII (Papa Luna), with sweeping sea views from its ramparts.
Get lost in the whitewashed, flower-lined alleys of the walled old quarter, many of which appeared in the TV series Game of Thrones.

activities.text: Walk the city walls and Bufador
activities.detail: Follow the ancient fortified walls to the Bufador, a natural blowhole in the rocks where waves crash dramatically during rough seas.
Spend an afternoon on this long sandy beach with calm, shallow waters and full views of the castle and old town skyline.

activities.text: Visit the Ermita de la Virgen de la Ermitana
activities.detail: Hike or drive up to this hilltop hermitage just outside town for panoramic coastal views and a peaceful pilgrimage site.
Enjoy a sunset walk along the seafront promenade lined with palm trees, restaurants, and views back toward the illuminated castle.

activities.text: Boat trip along the Costa del Azahar
activities.detail: Take a short boat excursion to see the peninsula and castle from the water, especially striking at golden hour.

food.detail: A classic Valencian coastal rice dish cooked in fish stock and served with the seafood on the side, a specialty of Peñíscola's fishing tradition.
A hearty fisherman's stew of local catch, potatoes, and saffron broth, reflecting the town's deep ties to the sea.
Sweet, prized red prawns from the nearby waters, often served simply grilled or boiled to highlight their natural flavor.
The region's iconic rice dish with chicken, rabbit, and beans, widely available and best enjoyed at a seafront terrace.
A noodle-based cousin of paella made with short pasta and seafood, popular along this stretch of coast.
A refreshing tiger-nut drink paired with soft sugary pastries, a beloved Valencian sweet treat for hot afternoons.

culture.text: Respect the old town's residential character
culture.detail: Many houses within the walls are still lived in, so keep noise down in the evenings as you wander the narrow lanes.

culture.text: Visit the castle early or late
culture.detail: Arrive near opening time or a couple hours before closing to avoid tour groups and midday heat on the exposed ramparts.

culture.text: Time your visit around local fiestas
culture.detail: The Fiestas Patronales in September honor the Virgen de la Ermitana with processions, fireworks, and traditional bull-running events.

culture.text: Parking is limited near the old town
culture.detail: Use the designated lots outside the walls, since vehicle access to the historic center is restricted for most visitors.

places.name: Xàtiva
places.description: A historic town dominated by its twin castle perched on a ridge above the rooftops. Rich in Roman, Moorish, and Christian heritage, it offers stunning views and a charming old quarter.

activities.detail: Explore the twin castle complex, Castell Menor and Castell Major, linked by a walled ridge with sweeping views over the Costera plain.
Visit this Renaissance former grain exchange housing archaeological finds and the famous inverted portrait of Felipe V.
See the grand collegiate church, known locally as La Seu, with its Gothic and Baroque elements in the old quarter.
Stroll the old Jewish and Moorish quarter's narrow lanes beneath the castle for a sense of the town's layered past.
Stop by this iconic lion-fountain landmark that has been a meeting point in the town center for centuries.
Admire the Gothic facade of the Royal Hospital, one of the town's most striking medieval buildings.
Walk along the old bridges and irrigation channels of the Albuera area for a quieter, greener side of Xàtiva.
Head just outside town to this significant prehistoric archaeological cave site set in a scenic gorge.

food.detail: A hearty winter rice dish cooked with beans and turnips, a signature comfort food of the Costera region.
A traditional stew of local vegetables, pork, and rice simmered together in a clay pot.
A flatbread topped with tuna, tomato, and onion, popular as a snack or light lunch in local bakeries.
Sample local pastry shops for traditional almond and honey sweets tied to Valencian confectionery traditions.
Cool off with the classic Valencian tigernut drink paired with sweet elongated pastries, found in local cafés.

food.text: Local Costera olive oil and wine
food.detail: Try regional extra virgin olive oil and DO Valencia wines often featured in Xàtiva's traditional restaurants.

culture.text: Visit early or late for the castle
culture.detail: Arrive in the morning or late afternoon to avoid midday heat while climbing the steep path to the castle.
If visiting in August, expect the town's major annual fair with processions, markets, and local festivities.
The old quarter and castle paths involve cobblestones and steep inclines, so comfortable footwear is essential.
Many small shops and some sights close for a few hours in the early afternoon, typical of Spanish small-town rhythms.

places.name: Calpe
places.description: A lively seaside town dominated by the massive limestone Peñón de Ifach rock jutting into the sea. It offers beautiful beaches, a fishing harbor, and great hiking up the iconic rock.

activities.detail: Follow the trail and tunnel carved through the rock up to the summit of this 332-metre limestone outcrop for sweeping views over the Costa Blanca.
This long sandy beach lined with cafés and palm trees is the town's main stretch of shoreline, right beneath the Peñón.
Walk around this pink-tinged salt lagoon, a protected wetland where flamingos and other wading birds gather.
Stroll the old town's narrow whitewashed streets, medieval walls, and colourful street art near the Iglesia Antigua.

activities.text: Visit the fishing port and lonja
activities.detail: See the working harbour where the local fleet lands its catch and, in the late afternoon, watch the fast-paced fish auction.

activities.text: See the Baños de la Reina
activities.detail: Explore these Roman-era fish-salting pools and ruins cut into the rocks near the seafront.

food.detail: A classic Alicante-style rice cooked in fish stock and served separately from the seafood it's flavoured with, a Calpe fishing-town staple.
A local noodle version of paella, cooked with seafood and often a garlicky alioli on the side.
A hearty fishermen's stew of fresh local fish, potatoes, and a rich saffron-tinted broth.
A cold roasted vegetable salad of peppers, aubergine, and salt cod, typical of the Valencian coast.
Sample whatever fish or seafood was auctioned that day at the port, served simply grilled in the harbourside restaurants.

culture.detail: Start the ascent in the morning to avoid midday heat and crowds, and stick to the marked path as the rock is a protected natural park.
The lonja near the port holds a lively fish auction in the late afternoon that's worth watching even if you're not buying.
Many small shops and some restaurants close in the early afternoon before reopening in the evening, especially outside peak summer season.
Calpe's Moros y Cristianos festival in late October fills the streets with parades, costumes, and mock battles celebrating the town's history.

places.name: Dénia
places.description: A charming port town overlooked by its hilltop castle, known as the gateway to Ibiza and Formentera ferries. It boasts great beaches, a bustling marina, and renowned rice cuisine as Spain's Gastronomy Region capital.

activities.detail: Explore this hilltop fortress with Moorish and medieval layers offering sweeping views over the town, port, and Montgó mountain.
Wander the lively harbor where ferries depart for Ibiza and Formentera and fishing boats unload their daily catch.
Swim in the clear, rocky coves of this protected marine reserve just south of town, ideal for snorkeling among posidonia meadows.
Enjoy the long sandy stretch north of the port, backed by chiringuitos and calm shallow waters.
Trek the trails of this dramatic limestone massif that dominates Dénia's skyline, rewarding climbers with coastal panoramas.
Browse this bustling covered market for fresh red prawns, local produce, and a genuine taste of Denia daily life.
Get lost in the whitewashed lanes below the castle, dotted with tapas bars, boutiques, and historic churches.

food.detail: A rice dish cooked in fish stock and served separately from the seafood, a Dénia classic born from local fishermen's traditions.
The prized red shrimp caught off Dénia's coast, prized for its sweet flavor and often eaten simply boiled or grilled.
A noodle-based cousin of paella that originated in this region, typically made with seafood and a garlicky alioli on the side.
A rustic stew of eel, potatoes, garlic, and paprika, reflecting the Valencian coast's rich fishing heritage.
A cold roasted vegetable dish of peppers, eggplant, and onion dressed in olive oil, a refreshing local starter.

culture.detail: Since Dénia is the main port for Ibiza and Formentera, ferry tickets sell out fast in summer, so reserve ahead.
Restaurants often don't open for dinner until 8:30 or 9pm, following typical Spanish meal times.
Dénia holds UNESCO Creative City of Gastronomy status, so expect a strong local pride in rice dishes and seafood quality.

culture.text: Dress modestly for the castle and churches
culture.detail: As with many historic Spanish sites, cover shoulders and knees when visiting religious or heritage buildings.

places.name: Benidorm
places.description: A high-rise beach resort city famous for its skyscraper skyline and long golden beaches. It's the entertainment and nightlife capital of the Costa Blanca, packed with theme parks and lively promenades.

activities.detail: Stroll or sunbathe on this bustling golden-sand beach backed by Benidorm's iconic skyscraper skyline.
Take in panoramic sea views from this clifftop viewpoint in the old town separating Levante and Poniente beaches.
Ride roller coasters through ancient-civilization themed zones at one of Spain's biggest amusement parks.
Take a glass-bottom boat to this small rocky island for snorkeling and views back at the coastline.
Cool off on slides and wave pools at one of Europe's most popular water parks.
Wander the whitewashed old town's narrow lanes, small squares, and the Sant Jaume church perched above the sea.
Bar-hop along the British-style pub zone and cabaret venues that make Benidorm the Costa Blanca's party capital.

food.detail: A Valencian-style rice dish cooked in fish stock and served separately from the seafood it's flavored with.
A noodle-based cousin of paella, typically made with shrimp, squid, and other seafood.
Flat savory pastries topped with vegetables, tuna, or sardines, popular as a quick snack.
A sweet tiger-nut drink paired with soft sugary pastries, best enjoyed at a terrace café.
Reflecting the huge British expat and tourist presence, many strip cafés serve classic fry-ups all day.

food.text: Tapas crawl in the old town
food.detail: Sample small plates like patatas bravas and jamón at traditional bars tucked into the historic quarter.

culture.detail: Due to massive British and northern European tourism, menus and signage are often in English alongside Spanish.
Restaurants fill up from around 9pm onward, following typical Spanish dining hours.
Colored flags indicate swimming safety conditions and are strictly enforced by lifeguards.
Benidorm is geared toward lively mass tourism, so expect a party atmosphere rather than a sleepy Spanish village vibe.

places.name: Xàbia
places.description: An elegant coastal town with dramatic cliffs, the iconic Granadella cove, and a charming old quarter. It combines upscale beach life with stunning natural scenery along the Costa Blanca.

activities.detail: Swim in the turquoise waters of this pine-backed cove often ranked among Spain's most beautiful beaches, best visited early morning to beat the crowds.

activities.text: Cap de Sant Antoni lighthouse walk
activities.detail: Walk or cycle the cliffside path to this lighthouse and nature reserve for sweeping views over the Mediterranean and Montgó massif.
Wander narrow stone streets around the fortified Gothic church of Sant Bartomeu, built partly for defense against pirate raids.
Climb the distinctive Montgó massif for panoramic views stretching from Xàbia to Dénia and the open sea.
Stroll the harbor with its fishing boats and continue to the Arenal beach strip lined with cafes and palm trees.
Kayak or hike to this dramatic hand-carved coastal cave accessible only by sea or a rugged cliffside trail.

activities.text: Museu Arqueològic i Etnogràfic Soler Blasco
activities.detail: Learn about the town's Iberian, Roman, and maritime history in this small museum housed in a historic building.

food.detail: A classic Valencian coastal rice dish cooked in fish stock, typically served with the seafood on the side.
A noodle-based cousin of paella made with short pasta and local seafood, often finished with alioli.
A cold roasted vegetable dish of peppers, eggplant, and salt cod, dressed simply with olive oil.
A traditional fisherman's stew of boiled local fish and potatoes, usually served with alioli on the side.
Xàbia's hillsides are historically known for sun-dried raisins and sweet moscatell wine, a legacy of its agricultural past.
Sample just-landed prawns, red mullet, and cuttlefish at the small restaurants near the fishing harbor.

culture.detail: Xàbia has a historic hilltop old town and a separate beachfront port area, so orient yourself before planning your day.
Parking at Cala Granadella is restricted in summer, so arrive very early or use the shuttle bus service.
Both Valencian (Catalan) and Spanish are used locally, with Xàbia and Jávea both being accepted names for the town.
Xàbia attracts a more relaxed, affluent crowd than nearby party towns, so nightlife is low-key and dining leans toward long, leisurely meals.

restaurants.name: Casa Montaña
restaurants.description: Legendary standing-room-only tapas bar in a historic building serving traditional Spanish bites with an extensive wine list.
restaurants.mustOrder: Boquerones en vinagre with local wines

restaurants.name: Horchatería Santa Catalina
restaurants.description: Historic horchata and fartons shop dating to 1836, perfect for a sweet Valencian experience.

restaurants.name: Restaurante Navarro
restaurants.description: Contemporary restaurant blending traditional Valencian ingredients with innovative techniques in an intimate setting.
restaurants.mustOrder: All i pebre (traditional eel and potato stew)

restaurants.name: Casa Montaña Vertical
restaurants.description: Sister establishment to Casa Montaña offering elevated tapas in a more comfortable seated environment.
restaurants.mustOrder: Jamón ibérico with pan con tomate

restaurants.name: El Pepica
restaurants.description: Beachfront paella restaurant with direct sea views, serving traditional rice dishes and fresh catch daily.

restaurants.name: Casa Montaña
restaurants.description: Family-run establishment in the historic heart of Xàtiva serving regional specialties with local charm.
restaurants.mustOrder: Espinacas con pasas y piñones (spinach with raisins and pine nuts)

restaurants.name: Vertical
restaurants.description: Avant-garde fine dining restaurant offering a creative tasting menu in a sleek, minimalist setting.
restaurants.mustOrder: Chef's tasting menu with wine pairings

restaurants.name: Palmira
restaurants.description: Beachfront classic known for authentic paellas and fresh seafood prepared simply and well.
restaurants.mustOrder: Arroz a banda (rice cooked in fish stock)

restaurants.name: Los Pescaítos
restaurants.description: Contemporary seafood restaurant celebrating the Mediterranean catch with elegant preparations.

restaurants.name: Casa Pacheco
restaurants.description: Historic restaurant in wine country serving hearty regional dishes paired with local Requena wines.
restaurants.mustOrder: Rabo de toro (oxtail stew) with Requena wine

restaurants.name: La Pepica
restaurants.description: Right on the beach beneath the historic castle, serving contemporary Mediterranean seafood with dramatic sea views.

restaurants.name: Ricard Camarena
restaurants.description: Michelin-starred restaurant pushing culinary boundaries with technical innovation and Valencian roots.

restaurants.name: Casa Montaña
restaurants.description: Mountain town establishment serving authentic regional cuisine in a warm, traditional setting.

restaurants.name: El Tollo
restaurants.description: Beach restaurant famous for paella and fresh catch, serving the Valencia coast for generations.

restaurants.name: Oca Bruja
restaurants.description: Contemporary restaurant celebrating seasonal vegetables and local ingredients with artistic presentation.
